# The Movemental Genius Knowledge Base: Phase 3

*Phase 3 of four ready phases: the Scenii model, and proof, evidence, and honest current status. Written only from Movemental's own documents, current as of May 2026. Each entry is written to stand on its own when retrieved. The strategic framing in Part III is Movemental's own analysis; the agent relays it as the company's reasoning. Items marked [LIVE] must be checked against the live source before being stated as settled.*

---

## Part III: The Scenii model (what is built for leaders)

### Scenius and Scenii, defined
Movemental uses two related words with care. A scenius is Brian Eno's term for the collective intelligence of a scene, the thing a community knows together that no single member could produce alone; it cannot be engineered or manufactured, only hosted. Scenii is Movemental's own word for what it builds: interoperable digital authority ecosystems that make an already-real movement structurally visible online, with each participating organization keeping its mission and identity while contributing to shared semantic infrastructure. The deliberate language is "credibility networks, not platforms." The scenius is the thing being served; a Scenii is the architecture that serves it. Source: Scenii strategic frame; origin and trajectory note.

### The four layers, not to be confused
Movemental insists on keeping four layers distinct. The scenius is the thing being served, the collective relational, intellectual, and practical intelligence of the network; it is not the platform and is not Movemental's to own. The corpus is the authors' own work, their books, articles, sermons, talks, and podcasts, owned by them and remediated only with their consent. The platform is the infrastructure that lets a distributed scene act like a scene: content management, learning management, a cohort engine, multi-tenant linking, and a translation layer. AI is the interpretive layer, the only technology now able to read millions of fragmented pre-existing words alongside the corpus and reflect the network back to itself coherently. Source: origin and trajectory note.

### Credibility networks, not platforms
Movemental's reasoning for selling networks rather than platforms is this: most of its customers already belong to movements. Offline they reinforce each other constantly, sharing audiences, theology, practitioners, language, and partnerships. Online they look like strangers, with separate websites, donor funnels, search footprints, and libraries. The relationship graph that animates the movement is invisible to the systems that now mediate authority, and in an AI-mediated internet that invisibility is not a marketing problem but an existential one, because a single platform, however well built, is lost in the torrent of slop. So Movemental stopped offering platforms as the primary unit of work and sells convened networks instead, with each organization keeping its identity and only the structural relationship between them changing. Source: Scenii strategic frame.

### What a Scenius is, operationally
Operationally, a Scenius is a coordinated ecosystem of organizations, leaders, educators, practitioners, storytellers, and institutions whose digital platforms reinforce one another through shared semantic infrastructure, AI-assisted publishing, interoperable systems, intentional authority architecture, and coordinated ontology, schema, and cross-linking. Movemental holds that the network effect begins at five nodes. Five is not a marketing number; it is the company's stated minimum at which cross-reinforcement begins to dominate any single node's reach. Below five, the result is still a federation of websites. At five and above, it becomes a distributed institution. Source: Scenii strategic frame.

### The five-node canonical anatomy
Movemental describes a five-node pattern, illustrated with a Christian foster-care network. Node 01 is the Cause-Centered Hub, the canonical authority hub that holds the field's vocabulary, training pathways, and movement-wide statistics. Node 02 is the Theological or Visionary Voice, the worldview and framework layer that gives the ecosystem intellectual coherence. Node 03 is Practitioner Implementation, where ideas become measurable field practice. Node 04 is Church Mobilization, where the movement meets local congregations through sermon kits, curriculum, and partnership playbooks. Node 05 is Storytelling and Narrative, the lived-experience layer that humanizes the network and that AI retrieval systems and human audiences reward most heavily. Each node keeps its own identity; the durable value sits in the cross-connections, not in a hub-and-spoke. Source: Scenii strategic frame; visible evidences.

### The visible evidences, on the public front end
Movemental documents what structurally distinguishes a Scenii from five separate websites, on the side a reader can see. Long-form articles carry reciprocal citation strips ("Cited in field by," "Mobilized through," "Witnessed in") that link to the practitioner, church, and story nodes. Shared glossary tooltips show one canonical definition across every node. A single federated search returns results from all nodes at once. Articles carry cross-node author bylines, and topic pages render the same canonical content in each host's chrome. A "movement view" toggle lets a reader read the whole ecosystem as one publication. Learning pathways, event surfaces, press kits, a unified movement footer, and coordinated semantic metadata complete the picture. The chrome is automatic, populated by the shared ontology. Source: visible evidences.

### The visible evidences, on the dashboard
On the operator side, Movemental describes instruments that read the network as a network. The Network Map shows edge density between nodes and flags underconnected pairs as connection opportunities. The Authority Convergence View ranks the topics on which the network is gaining or losing semantic authority. The Shared Ontology Editor lets the hub propose canonical terms that peers ratify, propagating across all sites within hours. A Cross-Node Editorial Calendar coordinates publishing, a Movement-Wide Author Directory finds co-authors, and a Cross-Node Resource Library indexes everything. Network Health Indicators read structural integrity rather than ordinary analytics. The Discernment Memo View shares AI-use adjudication across the network so it learns once and governs everywhere, and Provenance and Authorship Logs make AI assistance visible on every piece. Source: visible evidences.

### Why the evidences matter
Movemental's point about the visible evidences is that almost none of them can be produced by a freelancer building five separate sites. The cross-node citation chrome requires shared schema; the unified search requires a federated index; the glossary tooltips require canonical ontology with a ratification flow; the convergence view requires graph-level analytics across all nodes. Each one requires the other four nodes to exist and to have agreed to the architecture. They are therefore not features but evidence that the ecosystem is structurally real. In the company's phrase, the ecosystem looks like a network that knows itself, while the single node looks like a website that does not know what room it is in. Source: visible evidences.

### Move one: build one platform that does the whole job
Movemental's mechanism begins with one author, in this case Alan Hirsch, and builds the full apparatus his public work requires, not a website. That apparatus makes his entire body of work navigable around the pillars of his thinking; runs a content engine that produces new pieces at his depth and in his voice, anchored in his corpus and never inventing positions he has not taken; offers guided pathways and cohort-based courses; maintains a newsletter; and includes an AI conversation agent bounded to his corpus. The technical standard the company names in plain language is whether the platform demonstrates that the author has actually done the work, knows it deeply, is recognized by peers, and can be trusted to tell the truth. For Alan that is already true offline; the platform makes it legible online. Source: how-it-actually-works companion.

### Move two: build a second and link them
The second move builds a second platform, for Brad Brisco, on the same architecture, in a fraction of the time. The new thing it makes possible is linking. When Alan's work cites Brad's on a specific topic, the citation is a structural declaration that one author corroborates another, and the reverse runs when Brad cites Alan; shared canonical definitions add more. The result is not two websites but two credibility nodes that publicly reinforce each other. Movemental's stated reason this matters is that the systems mediating the internet evaluate networks, not isolated pages, and authority transfers through links between credible sources. It is the digital expression of an old truth: we trust people partly because of who vouches for them. Source: how-it-actually-works companion.

### Move three: multiply to a hundred with shared infrastructure
The third move repeats the build toward a hundred senior voices. Movemental states this is possible now because AI-assisted engineering dropped the per-platform cost from roughly $250,000 to $750,000 down to about $50,000, with coordination cost compressing too. What matters at scale is the shared infrastructure: a shared vocabulary with canonical definitions, a shared machine-readable schema, shared citation conventions, shared formation disciplines, and shared refusals. The formation discipline is a four-question test applied to every artifact: where is the dissonance, what is the action, what is the reflection structure, and where is community gestured at. These elements are encoded into the platform itself, so the discipline is structural rather than procedural, and a platform on the network cannot easily produce a low-credibility artifact. Source: how-it-actually-works companion.

### Move four: link them as a network the internet can see
The fourth move completes the architecture by operating the hundred nodes as one linked network with semantic density. Movemental's stated consequence: after the network exists for two to three years and accumulates the corroboration density of a hundred credible voices teaching the same body of work in coordinated form, the answer an AI returns to a question like "how do I plant a reproducing church" becomes the network's answer, not because retrieval was manipulated but because the real work was made structurally visible. The same holds across languages. New collaborations also form across nodes that did not exist before, and the hundred begin to behave online the way they have always behaved offline, as a real movement in active relationship. Source: how-it-actually-works companion.

### The hundred and the digital scenius
Movemental gathers roughly one hundred senior movement leaders because it believes this is approximately the right number to constitute a digital scenius. The hundred is not a directory, a conference, or a publisher; it is the seed of a working ecosystem in which a leader's work and the work of ninety-nine peers can find each other, link, deepen each other, and present coherently to the world and to the AI systems the world increasingly asks. The network is capped at one hundred by design, framed as care rather than scarcity-as-tactic. [LIVE] The current committed count is variable across documents and must be checked against the latest source. Source: letter to Movement Voices; origin and trajectory note; Praxis deck.

### What a Movement Voice is
Movemental is precise that a Movement Voice is not a testimonial or an advisor but a node in the network being built. Each Voice is, at once, a customer, a content contributor to the corpus, a relational connector to the next Voice, and part of the credibility signal that makes the network discoverable. This is also why the company frames the relational substrate as a moat: a competitor cannot start with the hundredth Voice, but has to start with the first, and the first has already chosen Movemental. Each Voice signed is a tile in a substrate no one else can lay. Source: Scenii strategic frame; Ferguson memo.

### What a Voice says yes to
Movemental describes participation plainly to incoming Voices. A Voice says yes to a four-week onboarding in which their existing life-work, the books, articles, sermons, talks, and scattered fragments, is remediated into a coherent home. For most leaders that looks like roughly fifty evergreen articles, five thematic pathway courses (one for each major stream of their work), and guided pathways through their core thinking. Participation is designed to cost no more than three to four hours per week. A Voice does not say yes to becoming a content creator or to outsourcing their voice. AI is used to do the remediation; it is never used to author. Every word that goes out under the Voice's name is theirs. Source: letter to Movement Voices; Ferguson memo.

### The diversity-of-the-Kingdom commitment
Movemental has committed, on the record, that the hundred will reflect the diversity of the Kingdom across geography, language, theological stream, tradition, gender, and generation. The company names the specific risk that the founders' own existing relational graphs could pull the hundred toward homogeneity, and states that if that gravity asserts itself, it will slow down. This is named as the one condition under which everything else is delayed, and the company frames it not as a marketing line but as the Pentecost pattern enacted structurally, the same reason the multilingual move matters. Source: letter to Movement Voices; origin and trajectory note; fragmentation thesis.

### The Evergreen Engine
The Evergreen Engine is the per-leader content system that produces a platform's library, roughly fifty evergreen articles and five pathway pillars, from one standardized, corpus-grounded method. Movemental is precise that the Engine does not invent authority; it translates authority that already exists into the formats discovery systems are built to read, because a book is a sealed container that search and answer engines cannot cite. The method is governed by three frameworks, including the EEAT content-quality standard, and runs through ten article archetypes and nine pathway components, with an editorial gate at the end. AI drafts the structure; the human authors the meaning. Source: The Evergreen Engine guide, Edition One, 2026.

### The strategic thesis, in one sentence
Movemental's own one-sentence statement of strategy: it does not build platforms, it builds Scenii, interoperable digital authority ecosystems that make an already-real movement structurally visible online, with each participating organization retaining its mission and identity while contributing to shared semantic infrastructure. The company treats this as a distinct category from "a website" or "a content platform," and frames its sales conversation as competing on category rather than on price. Source: Scenii strategic frame.

### Why this is a moat
Movemental names three independent reasons it considers the work defensible despite no traditional technological defensibility. First, venture-capital unit economics forbid the strategy: networks of five to twenty mission-aligned organizations cannot be modeled as a venture-scale market, so the thing that makes Scenii valuable is the same thing that makes them unfundable as a venture company. Second, tech companies lack the relational substrate and cannot fake it, because the prerequisite is being trusted by senior figures to convene the nodes, and that trust is not purchasable. Third, tech companies are optimized to replace relational labor, not steward it, and credibility is constituted by relational labor, so automating it away destroys what it claims to provide. Source: Scenii strategic frame.

### The economic shift behind Scenii
Movemental grounds its claim that Scenii are newly possible in a specific change in cost. Before AI-assisted development, building this category of infrastructure ran roughly $250,000 to $750,000 or more per organization, and $1.5M to $5M or more for a coordinated five-organization ecosystem. With AI-native engineering, the company puts the figures at about $50,000 per organization and about $250,000 for a five-org ecosystem. Its stated point is that the historical price was never a function of value but of coordination cost: engineering teams, CMS architects, SEO consultants, LMS and CRM integration, and semantic architecture, all coordinated across five interoperable platforms. AI-native engineering compresses that, which is what makes the model reachable for mission-driven organizations for the first time. Source: Scenii strategic frame.

### The freelance economy as evangelist
Movemental's stated view of the freelance "context-coder" building cheap single-org sites is that she is not a competitor but an unwitting evangelist. Every inexpensive site shipped into a movement is a node that should have been part of a Scenii and is not; within months it is invisible to AI retrieval and indistinguishable from slop, and the organization concludes "the website thing did not work," which is what brings it to Movemental. The company is explicit about not punching down: a freelancer building good single-org sites is doing real work, often for organizations that cannot yet afford anything else, and Movemental says it can integrate what is good about an existing site into the Scenii architecture later. Source: Scenii strategic frame.

### The window, and the translation roadmap
Movemental frames a roughly twelve to eighteen month window in which serious technical talent is not yet building Scenii because the category is not yet visible. Every Voice signed in that window is substrate that is functionally non-transferable, so the lead is built during the window. The translation roadmap extends the same logic: once the English-language hundred is built, the corpus moves into Brazilian Portuguese, Latin American Spanish, Mandarin, Korean, German, and French, turning a hundred English-speaking seniors into the seed of an international ecosystem whose peer networks mirror the original DNA in their own contexts. The translation move is both the Pentecost pattern and a temporal moat, because each language ecosystem accrues additional non-transferable substrate. Source: Scenii strategic frame; origin and trajectory note.

### The GEO and authorship signal
Movemental's term for the long-run effect is the GEO move, and it is careful to call it downstream of credibility rather than a substitute for it. Every published article, every linked corpus, every cross-cited Voice, and every schema-coordinated page contributes to a coherent semantic signal that AI retrieval systems weight increasingly heavily. After a few years of compounding signal, the network's authority on missional church, movement thinking, and redemptive technology for mission becomes the answer the AI returns. The company stresses that this is reclaiming authorship of how movement thinking gets answered, not manipulating retrieval, and that it works only because the underlying work was real. Source: Scenii strategic frame; origin and trajectory note.

### The North Star, and the questions the frame forces
Movemental states its own North Star in seven words: "Always be building Scenii. It is the work." The strategic frame closes by forcing three questions the company holds for itself. Why would it ever build just one platform again, given who its customers are and the movements they already belong to? What if Scenii are how major institutions platform marginalized voices, not as content guests but as structurally integrated authority nodes? And what if a major part of the calling is to champion intentional scenius-building as the only durable way forward in an AI-mediated internet? The agent can offer these as the questions Movemental uses to keep itself oriented. Source: Scenii strategic frame.

---

## Part XI: Proof, evidence, and honest current status

### The honest current state
The agent should be candid about what is real now versus forthcoming. The four stages of the Path are built. The Field Guides for Safety and Sandbox are available; the Skills and Solutions guides are forthcoming. The Path Bundle discount and the under-resourced access mechanism are not yet finalized. Case studies are described by the company as honest and in development, with named studies not yet published; the audience pages use reconstructed rather than named cases. [LIVE] The committed Movement Voice count is variable; the Praxis deck states twenty-five committed against the cap of one hundred. The Praxis deck itself carries bracketed placeholders, such as "[X] leader charters drafted." The agent never quotes a placeholder as a fact. Source: Praxis deck; site map; /pricing copy prompt.

### The claims-and-sources discipline
Movemental maintains a public "Claims and sources" page at `/footnotes` that backs the statistics used across the site, with individual citation anchors. When asked for the evidence behind a site statistic, the agent points to this page rather than restating the figure as its own. The site also distinguishes an `/evidence` page, which explains the problem structure the company addresses, from the Voices, who validate that the problem is real. The agent keeps that distinction: evidence explains the problem; the Voices are the proof of standing. Source: site map.

### Confirmed Voices versus named targets
The agent distinguishes carefully between leaders who are confirmed Voices and leaders named only as illustrations or aspirations. Individual profile pages exist for Liz Ríos, JR Woodward, and Rowland Smith, who are therefore the leaders most likely to be confirmed participants; verify against the live `/voices` pages. Michael T. Cooper appears as the worked example for the leader-profile dashboard. Other senior leaders are named in the documents only as illustrative targets of the kind of person the network is for, and Dave Ferguson is explicitly someone the company is approaching, not a committed Voice; the Ferguson memo states it is not asking him to join. The agent does not describe any leader as a committed Voice unless a current source confirms it. [LIVE] Source: site map; how-it-actually-works companion; Ferguson memo; How Movemental Uses AI inventory.

### The reference build as proof of capacity
Movemental points to the Alan Hirsch platform as evidence that the technical capacity claim is real rather than aspirational. The strategic frame describes a reference build of roughly four hundred thousand lines of TypeScript across the front end and dashboard. The system inventory describes the Alan Hirsch tenant as the largest deployed application, with dozens of wired agents, and describes the whole system as built by one person, Joshua Shepherd, context-coding with Cursor and Claude Code between roughly September 2025 and April 2026. This is the basis for the claim that Scenii are now buildable at the new price point. The agent presents these as the company's own figures from its own inventory, and notes that specifics change. Source: Scenii strategic frame; How Movemental Uses AI inventory.

### What proof is still outstanding
The agent should name plainly what the company has not yet proven publicly. The network's compounding authority effect is a multi-year claim that has not yet had years to compound. The hundred is not yet assembled. The translation ecosystems do not yet exist. Named, published case studies of organizations that walked the Path are not yet available. These are stated by the company as its trajectory, not as accomplished results, and the agent represents them that way: as where the work is going, supported by the reference build and the early commitments, not as finished evidence. Source: origin and trajectory note; Praxis deck; site map.

---

*Phase 3 of the knowledge base. Phase 4 (current AI reality, how Movemental uses AI, and the product surface) is ready to write from documents in hand. Phase 5 stays blocked until the image-only Playbooks and Participation Agreement are read and the live `/voices`, `/faq`, `/assess`, `/how-we-use-ai`, and `/movement-voice-commitments` pages are checked.*
